summary: McKenzie describes implementing a 'popular bingo cards' feature on his Daily Bingo Cards site, which generated about 300 downloads across roughly 100 cards with minimal promotion. He references the long tail distribution visible in the download stats, where a few top pages dominate while many others contribute smaller amounts. The post also includes a candid aside about broken caching logic causing slow page loads due to Ruby iterating over hundreds of objects. It's a brief, informal update mixing product development with technical debt acknowledgment.
key_points: A simple 'popular items' feature generated 300 downloads across ~100 cards with very little promotion in two weeks
Download distribution follows a long tail pattern, with top pages capturing disproportionate traffic
Technical debt is acknowledged but deprioritized — broken caching causes slow page generation but isn't blocking users significantly
money_quotes: If you want to see the distribution of how many of those are due to the top five pages and how many are not, why not mosey on over.
Yeah, I know, I know, fix the cache or do the math at the DB, but I'm lazy and its late.
key_insight: Even a simple content library with minimal promotion reveals long tail distribution patterns, where a few popular items drive most of the traffic while the rest collectively contribute meaningful volume.
